1: An allusion: 'You shall not make idols for yourselves or erect an image or pillar.' The idol prohibition opens the covenant's blessing-and-curse section — the first commandment grounds the whole. Christ is the true image: 'He is the image (eikon) of the invisible God' (Colossians 1:15). The prohibition against crafted images is the negative side of which Christ's incarnation is the positive: the uncraftable, unimaginable God has given humanity his own true image in the Son.
2: An allusion: 'You shall keep my Sabbaths and reverence my sanctuary: I am YHWH.' Christ is Lord of the Sabbath (Mark 2:28; Matthew 12:8) — not merely a keeper of the Sabbath command but its source and fulfillment. 'The Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath' — Christ reinterprets the Sabbath's purpose as his own to define. His resurrection on the first day reshapes the Sabbath into the Lord's Day, the new-creation Sabbath.
3: An allusion: 'If you walk in my statutes and observe my commandments and do them...' — the conditional clause that opens the blessing section. Christ is the only one who fully met this condition. His perfect obedience (Philippians 2:8; Hebrews 5:8-9) is the ground on which the blessings of Leviticus 26:4-13 flow to his people. The conditionality of Leviticus 26 is not abolished but fulfilled: Christ fulfills the condition so that those in him receive the blessings unconditionally.
4: An allusion: the rain in its season and the harvest blessing follow covenant faithfulness. Christ's perfect obedience to the Father brings the harvest of the redeemed: 'Unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit' (John 12:24). The agricultural harvest of Leviticus 26:4 is the natural type; the harvest of souls gathered by Christ's death and resurrection is the spiritual fulfillment.
5: An allusion: threshing reaching to the grape harvest, grape harvest reaching to the sowing — unending abundance following obedience. Amos 9:13 echoes this vision: 'The plowman shall overtake the reaper.' Christ's kingdom is described in harvest terms throughout the Gospels (Matthew 9:37-38; Leviticus 13:23; John 4:35-36). The abundance of Leviticus 26:5 is the type of the kingdom harvest that knows no end.
6: A direct type: 'I will give peace in the land... and the sword shall not go through your land.' Christ is 'the Prince of Peace' (Isaiah 9:6) who 'made peace by the blood of his cross' (Colossians 1:20). 'He himself is our peace, who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility' (Ephesians 2:14). The peace of the blessed land in Leviticus 26:6 — security from enemies, harmony in the community — is the type of the comprehensive shalom Christ brings.
7: An allusion: five chasing a hundred, the miraculous multiplication of power in victory. Christ's disciples went out as 'sheep among wolves' (Matthew 10:16) — the ratio was impossibly against them — yet the early church's expansion shows precisely this pattern: small numbers producing disproportionate impact under the Spirit's power (Acts 2:41; Leviticus 4:4).
8: An allusion: 'Five of you shall chase a hundred, and a hundred of you shall chase ten thousand, and your enemies shall fall before you by the sword.' The disproportionate victory covenant. Christ promises the same to his church: 'I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it' (Matthew 16:18). The gates of Hades — the defensive fortifications of death itself — cannot withstand the advance of Christ's people.
9: An allusion: 'I will turn to you and make you fruitful and multiply you and confirm my covenant with you.' The Abrahamic fruitfulness promise renewed through covenant obedience. Christ as the seed of Abraham through whom all nations are blessed (Galatians 3:16); the church as the Abrahamic family multiplied beyond reckoning (Galatians 3:29). The new covenant 'confirms' (establishes firmly, Hebrews 9:15-17) what the old covenant anticipated.
10: An allusion: 'You shall eat old store long kept, and you shall clear out the old to make way for the new.' The old harvest cleared to make room for the new. The new covenant making way by superseding — not destroying — the old: 'What is becoming obsolete and growing old is ready to vanish away' (Hebrews 8:13). Christ does not patch the old garment; he brings the new wine that requires new wineskins (Matthew 9:16-17).
11: A direct type: 'I will make my dwelling (mishkan) among you, and my soul shall not abhor you.' The divine dwelling among Israel is the tabernacle promise. John 1:14 fulfills it: 'The Word became flesh and tabernacled (eskēnōsen) among us.' The same root (shakan/mishkan) underlies both the tabernacle and John's incarnation description. In Christ the mishkan promise becomes literal: God's presence is not in a tent but in a human body.
12: A direct fulfillment: 'And I will walk among you and will be your God, and you shall be my people.' The foundational covenant formula. Paul quotes this verse in 2 Corinthians 6:16 as the basis for the church's identity as the temple of the living God: 'For we are the temple of the living God; as God said: I will make my dwelling among them and walk among them.' The new covenant community is the fulfillment of the walking-among-you promise — YHWH tabernacling in his Spirit-indwelt people.
13: An allusion: 'I am YHWH your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, that you should not be their slaves. And I have broken the bars of your yoke and made you walk erect.' Christ's easy yoke replaces the crushing yoke: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and lowly in heart, and you will find rest for your souls. For my yoke is easy, and my burden is light' (Matthew 11:28-30). He broke the bars of the law's condemning power (Galatians 3:13; Colossians 2:14) to make the redeemed walk erect in dignity.
14: An allusion: 'But if you will not listen to me and will not do all these commandments...' — the curse section begins. This is the legal framework within which Christ's atoning work operates: the curse is real; the covenant threatened real consequences. Galatians 3:10: 'All who rely on works of the law are under a curse, for it is written: Cursed is everyone who does not abide by all things written in the Book of the Law and do them.' The 'if' of Leviticus 26:14 becomes the 'since' of human reality; the 'but' of grace enters in Christ.
15: An allusion: rejecting YHWH's statutes and breaking the covenant — this is the condition that makes the curse operative. The ultimate rejection of the covenant was the rejection of Christ himself (John 1:11). Yet in a stunning reversal, the one rejected became the bearer of the rejection's consequences: 'He was despised and rejected by men' (Isaiah 53:3) precisely in order that those who rejected him might be forgiven.
16: An allusion: the first curse tier — diseases, wasting, fever, eyes consumed, life drained. Christ took on all the infirmities of the curse: 'He took our illnesses and bore our diseases' (Matthew 8:17, quoting Isaiah 53:4). The healing ministry of Jesus was not incidental to his mission but its enacted sign: he absorbed the curse's symptoms in his own body and began reversing them in others, previewing the total reversal of the curse at the resurrection.
17: An allusion: 'I will set my face against you, and you shall be struck down before your enemies.' The curse of divine opposition — YHWH's face turned against rather than toward. Christ on the cross experienced the ultimate form of this: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' (Matthew 27:46). The God-abandonment of Psalm 22 is the total form of what Leviticus 26:17 threatens: the covenant God turning his face away.
18: An allusion: 'And if in spite of this you will not listen to me, then I will discipline you again sevenfold for your sins.' The sevenfold escalation of judgment. Christ bore not a minimal judgment but a complete one — the full weight of divine wrath against sin (Romans 3:25-26; 1 John 2:2). The 'sevenfold' completeness of the curse in Leviticus 26:18, 21, 24, 28 corresponds to the completeness of atonement Christ made: 'It is finished' (John 19:30).
19: An allusion: 'I will break the pride of your power, and I will make your heavens like iron and your earth like bronze.' The arrogance that produces covenant failure is crushed. Christ, by contrast, 'humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross' (Philippians 2:8). His was the opposite of the pride that draws the curse — his self-emptying obedience is the ground of his people's exaltation (Philippians 2:9-11).
20: An allusion: 'And your strength shall be spent in vain, for your land shall not yield its increase, and the trees of the land shall not yield their fruit.' The futility curse — labor that produces nothing. Paul describes the creation's futility in similar terms (Romans 8:20-21) and the new creation's liberation from it. Christ's resurrection is the firstfruits of the liberation from futility: 'Your labor in the Lord is not in vain' (1 Corinthians 15:58).
21: An allusion: the second curse tier — the sevenfold plague escalation for ongoing disobedience. The graduated nature of the curse reflects the graduated nature of divine patience: YHWH gives opportunity after opportunity to repent before the full curse falls. Christ's ministry echoed this: 'O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the city that kills the prophets... How often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings, and you were not willing!' (Matthew 23:37).
22: An allusion: 'I will let loose the wild beasts against you, which shall bereave you of your children and destroy your livestock and make you few in number.' The created order turning against the covenant people. In contrast, when Christ faced his wilderness temptation he was 'with the wild animals, and the angels were ministering to him' (Mark 1:13) — a deliberate echo of Eden's lost harmony, restored in the new Adam. Where covenant failure unleashes wild beasts, Christ's presence restores the peaceable kingdom (Isaiah 11:6-9).
23: An allusion: 'And if by this discipline you are not turned to me but walk contrary to me...' — the refusal to repent even under judgment. The hardness of heart that refuses chastening was the spiritual condition of the generation that rejected Christ (John 12:40; Matthew 13:15). The curse section of Lev 26 traces the progressive hardening that ultimately required not more chastening but a wholly new redemptive act.
24: An allusion: 'Then I also will walk contrary to you, and I myself will strike you sevenfold for your sins.' The divine-contrary posture mirrors the human-contrary posture — the relationship has broken down completely. Christ came precisely at the point of this total relational breakdown: 'But when the fullness of time had come, God sent forth his Son, born of woman, born under the law, to redeem those who were under the law' (Galatians 4:4-5).
25: An allusion: 'I will bring a sword upon you, that shall execute vengeance for the covenant.' The sword of covenant enforcement — the judgment side of the legal covenant. Zechariah 13:7: 'Awake, O sword, against my shepherd, against the man who stands next to me.' Christ quoted this verse at Gethsemane (Matthew 26:31): the covenant sword fell on the shepherd rather than the sheep. The covenant's punishing sword was borne by the mediator.
26: A direct type: 'When I break your supply of bread, ten women shall bake your bread in a single oven and shall dole out your bread again by weight, and you shall eat and not be satisfied.' The curse of insufficient bread is the negative image of Christ's miraculous feeding: 'I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst' (John 6:35). Where the curse produces scarcity and dissatisfaction, Christ produces superabundance: 'They all ate and were satisfied, and they took up twelve baskets full of broken pieces' (Matthew 14:20).
27: An allusion: continued disobedience despite the staff of bread being broken — still not turning. The stubbornness of the human heart under judgment illustrates why the incarnation was necessary: no amount of external pressure produces the internal transformation only Christ can accomplish. 'I will give you a new heart and a new spirit I will put within you. And I will remove the heart of stone from your flesh and give you a heart of flesh' (Ezekiel 36:26).
28: An allusion: 'I will walk contrary to you in fury, and I myself will discipline you sevenfold for your sins.' The divine fury — the wrath of the covenant God against covenant-breaking. This is the wrath that Christ absorbed: 'God put him forward as a propitiation by his blood' (Romans 3:25). The fury of Leviticus 26:28 is the reality that makes propitiation necessary; Christ's blood is the answer the curse section anticipates.
29: An allusion: the extremity of the curse — cannibalism of sons and daughters, the ultimate sign of covenant breakdown. This was literally fulfilled in the sieges of Jerusalem (2 Kings 6:28-29; Lamentations 4:10) as Jesus foresaw the same for 70 CE (Luke 21:20-24). Christ wept over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41-44) knowing the curse's full weight would fall there. The depths of sin's consequences that Leviticus 26:29 describes are the depths into which Christ descended to redeem.
30: An allusion: 'I will destroy your high places and cut down your incense altars and cast your dead bodies upon the dead bodies of your idols, and my soul will abhor you.' The destruction of false worship. Christ triumphed over all false spiritual powers: 'He disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him' (Colossians 2:15). The cross dismantled the system of false mediation between humanity and God — the high places made redundant by the true high priest.
31: A direct type: 'And I will lay your cities waste and will make your sanctuaries desolate.' Christ predicted the literal fulfillment of this for the temple: 'There will not be left here one stone upon another that will not be thrown down' (Matthew 24:2; fulfilled 70 CE). But he also declared himself the replacement sanctuary: 'Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up... he was speaking about the temple of his body' (John 2:19-21). The desolated sanctuary is the shadow; Christ as the eternal temple is the fulfillment.
32: An allusion: 'And I myself will devastate the land, so that your enemies who settle in it shall be appalled by it.' The exile — Israel's land under desolation. The exile's desolation created the longing for the one who would restore: the prophetic hope generated in the exile (Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel) is the most Christologically rich period of the OT. The desolation of Leviticus 26:32 is what made Isaiah 40:1-2's 'Comfort, comfort my people' so urgent.
33: An allusion: 'And I will scatter you among the nations, and I will unsheathe the sword after you, and your land shall be a desolation, and your cities shall be a waste.' The Diaspora — Israel scattered. Paul interprets the Diaspora as part of YHWH's plan to bring the gospel to the nations: 'through their trespass salvation has come to the Gentiles' (Romans 11:11). The scattering becomes, paradoxically, the seed-spreading of YHWH's purposes.
34: An allusion: 'Then the land shall enjoy its Sabbaths as long as it lies desolate, while you are in your enemies' land; then the land shall rest, and enjoy its Sabbaths.' The enforced Sabbath rest during exile. Hebrews 4:1-11 develops the Sabbath-rest theology: 'There remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God' — the rest that was not fully achieved in the land-promise, the Canaan-rest pointing beyond itself to the rest in Christ. The land's exile-Sabbath anticipates the eternal rest.
35: An allusion: the land rests in exile, making up for the Sabbath years Israel did not observe. Christ's final rest — 'It is finished' (John 19:30) — is the ultimate Sabbath declaration: the work of redemption is complete, the debt is paid, the rest is secured. Hebrews 4:10: 'Whoever has entered God's rest has also rested from his works as God did from his.'
36: An allusion: 'As for those of you who are left, I will send faintness into their hearts in the lands of their enemies. The sound of a driven leaf shall put them to flight, and they shall flee as one flees from the sword.' The spiritual desolation of the scattered people — fear, disorientation, loss of identity. Christ came to gather the scattered: 'He gathers the outcasts of Israel' (Psalms 147:2); 'I will seek the lost, and I will bring back the strayed' (Ezekiel 34:16). The Great Shepherd comes for the frightened, faint-hearted exiles.
37: An allusion: 'They shall stumble over one another, as if to escape a sword, though none pursues.' The disarray of the cursed community — stumbling without even a real threat. Christ is the one who gathers the stumbling into a community that walks together: 'I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life' (John 8:12). The stumbling in darkness of Leviticus 26:37 contrasts with the sure-footed walking in light that Christ provides.
38: An allusion: 'And you shall perish among the nations, and the land of your enemies shall eat you up.' The threat of permanent assimilation and loss. YHWH's preservation of a remnant through exile was against all odds — precisely what made the return and eventual Messiah-coming possible. Christ came as the fulfillment of the preserved remnant: 'I did not come to abolish the Law or the Prophets but to fulfill them' (Matthew 5:17) — the remnant-bearer of all that the exile threatened to destroy.
39: An allusion: 'And those of you who are left shall rot away in your enemies' lands because of their iniquity, and also because of the iniquities of their fathers they shall rot away like them.' The inherited iniquity — the sins of fathers transmitted to children. Christ broke the cycle of inherited guilt: 'God made him who had no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God' (2 Corinthians 5:21). The generational rot ends where the one without inherited iniquity absorbs what humanity accumulated.
40: An allusion: 'But if they confess their iniquity and the iniquity of their fathers in their treachery... then I will remember my covenant.' The turning point — confession as the gateway to covenant restoration. 'If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness' (1 John 1:9). The restoration formula of Leviticus 26:40 is the shadow of the new covenant's forgiveness, grounded not in Israel's confession per se but in Christ's atoning work that makes confession efficacious.
41: A direct type: 'And walk contrary to me, so that I walked contrary to them and brought them into the land of their enemies — if then their uncircumcised heart is humbled...' The uncircumcised heart is the problem requiring not just external reform but internal transformation. Paul: 'No one is a Jew who is merely one outwardly, nor is circumcision outward and physical. But a Jew is one inwardly, and circumcision is a matter of the heart, by the Spirit, not by the letter' (Romans 2:28-29). Christ gives the circumcision of the heart (Colossians 2:11) that the covenant always required but could not itself produce.
42: A direct fulfillment: 'Then I will remember my covenant with Jacob, and I will remember my covenant with Isaac and my covenant with Abraham, and I will remember the land.' The Abrahamic covenant as the bedrock beneath the Sinai structure. Galatians 3:16: 'Now the promises were made to Abraham and to his offspring. It does not say, And to offsprings, referring to many, but to one, And to your offspring, who is Christ.' Christ is the specific seed through whom all three patriarchal covenant strands — Abraham, Isaac, Jacob — are fulfilled.
43: An allusion: the land enjoys its Sabbath in desolation because Israel did not observe the Sabbath years. Even in the judgment, the creation structure persists — YHWH's order cannot finally be violated without consequence. The Jubilee and Sabbath-year system that created this dynamic is what Paul has in mind when he speaks of the law as a custodian/guardian (Galatians 3:24-25): the covenant's legal structures held things in place until the fullness of time when Christ came to fulfill them.
44: A direct fulfillment: 'Yet for all that, when they are in the land of their enemies, I will not spurn them, neither will I abhor them so as to destroy them utterly and break my covenant with them, for I am YHWH their God.' The unconditional preservation promise embedded within the curses. Paul quotes the prophetic expression of this in Romans 11:26-29: 'All Israel will be saved... As regards election, they are beloved for the sake of their forefathers. For the gifts and calling of God are irrevocable.' YHWH's 'not destroy them utterly' of Leviticus 26:44 is the root of Paul's 'gifts and calling are irrevocable.'
45: A direct fulfillment: 'But I will for their sake remember the covenant with their forefathers, whom I brought out of the land of Egypt in the sight of the nations, that I might be their God: I am YHWH.' Hebrews 8:6: Christ 'is the mediator of a better covenant, which is enacted on better promises.' The covenant YHWH promised to remember in Leviticus 26:45 is ultimately the new covenant mediated by Christ — built on the better promises of Jeremiah 31:31-34, grounded in the better sacrifice of the Son. The 'I will remember' of Leviticus 26:45 is confirmed in Christ's blood: 'This cup is the new covenant in my blood' (1 Corinthians 11:25).
46: A direct type: 'These are the statutes and rules and laws that YHWH made between himself and the people of Israel through Moses on Mount Sinai.' The closing formula of the Holiness Code and the body of Leviticus. Christ: 'Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them' (Matthew 5:17). Every statute of the Holiness Code — the sacrifice regulations, the purity laws, the festival calendar, the blessings and curses — is fulfilled in Christ. He is the end (telos, goal/completion) of the law (Romans 10:4).
